g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lesson on Product Versus Process Quality Management in software engineering.
Quality management determines software success. Two perspectives exist: product quality focuses on the output, while process quality focuses on how we create it.
Historically, organizations tested products at the end to find defects. This reactive approach was costly. The shift toward process improvement changed everything.
g.text: Test final product to find defects

g.data-narration: Product quality focuses on the final software output - its functionality, reliability, performance, usability, and maintainability. It's what the customer experiences.
g.text: Primary Methods: Testing, Inspection, Quality Attributes Verification

g.data-narration: ISO 9126 is the international standard defining six quality characteristics. Each characteristic has sub-characteristics like suitability, accuracy, and security under functionality.
McCall's model organizes 11 quality factors into three categories: product operation, revision, and transition. It was influential in establishing multi-dimensional quality.
Process quality focuses on HOW software is created. The principle: high-quality processes lead to high-quality products. It emphasizes prevention over detection.
g.text: Key Principle: Improve the process - Better products consistently

g.data-narration: CMMI defines five maturity levels. Level 1 is ad-hoc, Level 5 is optimizing. Organizations progress from reactive to data-driven continuous improvement.
Other important process frameworks include ISO 9001 for documented quality systems, Six Sigma for reducing variation using DMAIC, and TQM for organization-wide quality culture.
Deming's 85/15 rule states that 85% of quality problems are due to processes, not people. This is the foundation of process-centric quality management.
Inspections are process-focused - they prevent defects from entering the product. Testing is product-focused - it finds failures caused by defects. Both are essential.
Product metrics include defect density (defects per KLOC). Process metrics include defect removal efficiency (percentage removed before release).
Modern quality management integrates both approaches. Process quality provides the foundation for reproducible outcomes, while product quality validates the results.
Modern practices like DevOps, continuous integration, and automated testing embody process quality principles. They prevent defects through systematic, automated workflows.
To summarize: Product quality focuses on the output characteristics. Process quality focuses on improving how we create the output. Both approaches are complementary and necessary.
